title: Stage Gates and Required Readiness
description: Understand the readiness criteria and gate requirements before moving opportunities to the next stage.

sections.title: Stage Gates and Required Readiness
sections.content:
One of the most important disciplines in the Salesforce commercial process is stage gate enforcement. A stage gate ensures that an opportunity cannot advance to the next stage until specific readiness criteria are met. Stage gates serve multiple purposes: they ensure that critical activities are completed before advancing, they prevent deals from being moved forward prematurely, and they help maintain data quality and forecasting accuracy.

Understand this principle: you do not move an opportunity to a new stage just because time has passed or because the sales representative wants to move it. You move an opportunity to a new stage only when the specific readiness criteria for that stage have been satisfied.

**Gate Requirements Before Moving to Qualification**:

To create an Opportunity (move from Lead to Qualification stage), the following must be in place:

- **Lead Converted**: The lead has been qualified and converted into the account hierarchy.
- **Account Established**: The Parent Account and Entity Child Account are confirmed and set up correctly.
- **Primary Contact Confirmed**: There is a defined primary contact who represents the customer and is authorized to engage with NxN.
- **Initial Need Understood**: The sales team has conducted at least an initial conversation with the customer and understands the basic business need or use case.

**Gate Requirements Before Moving to Solution Definition**:

Before an opportunity can advance from Qualification to Solution Definition, the following must be ready:

- **Primary Contact**: A specific, named primary contact must be identified and confirmed. This is the person the sales team will work with throughout the sales process.
- **Next Steps Defined**: The sales team must have identified and documented the next specific action (e.g., "Technical discovery call scheduled for March 15," "Customer to attend product demo on March 10").
- **Basic Value and Scope Understanding**: The sales team must have conducted initial discovery and understand, at a high level, what problem the customer is trying to solve and what scope of engagement might be relevant (e.g., "Customer wants to consolidate three legacy systems into one platform, estimated 50 users").
- **Timeline**: The customer must have provided or the team must have validated a realistic timeline for decision-making (e.g., "Customer expects to make a decision by June").

**Gate Requirements Before Moving to Pricing & Quotation**:

Before an opportunity can advance from Solution Definition to Pricing & Quotation, the following must be ready:

- **Products Identified**: The specific products and services that will be proposed have been identified. The sales team knows what they're going to quote.
- **Pricing Basis Documented**: The basis for pricing has been documented. Is it per-user? Per-transaction? Per-year? Flat-fee? The sales team must understand and have documented the pricing model before the quote is prepared.
- **Scope Locked**: The scope of the engagement is defined with sufficient detail that a quote can be prepared. The team knows how many users, what modules, what service level, what geography, etc.
- **Internal Alignment**: The sales team, account management team, and any product or technical specialists are aligned on the proposed solution and scope.
- **Customer Expectations Set**: The customer understands the general direction of the solution and has confirmed that they want to proceed to a detailed proposal stage.

**Gate Requirements Before Moving to Customer Review**:

Before an opportunity can advance from Pricing & Quotation to Customer Review, the following must be ready:

- **Quote Prepared**: A formal quote or proposal document has been prepared, including products, quantities, pricing, service level agreements, terms, and any special conditions.
- **Quote Approved**: The quote has been reviewed and approved by the appropriate approvers (pricing authority, account manager, or other gatekeepers as defined by company policy). A quote should never be sent to a customer without internal approval.
- **Customer Briefing Scheduled**: The sales team has scheduled a time to present or deliver the quote to the customer and is prepared to answer questions.

**Gate Requirements Before Moving to Contracting**:

Before an opportunity can advance from Customer Review to Contracting, the following must be ready:

- **Quote Accepted**: The customer has confirmed acceptance of the proposed pricing, scope, and general terms. If the customer has requested changes, the quote has been revised and approved, and the customer has accepted the revised version.
- **No Open Questions**: Any questions the customer raised during Customer Review have been answered to the customer's satisfaction.
- **Authority Confirmed**: The person who will be signing the contract (on the customer side) has been identified and confirmed to be authorized to execute commercial agreements.
- **Contracting Team Engaged**: The legal, contracts, or approvals team has been engaged and is prepared to prepare and manage the contract.

**Gate Requirements Before Closing Won**:

Before an opportunity can be closed as Won, the following must be ready:

- **Contract Signed or Acceptance Obtained**: The contract or purchase agreement has been executed by authorized representatives from both NxN and the customer. Alternatively, written acceptance evidence (e.g., a signed order confirmation, a purchase order, or an email confirmation) has been obtained.
- **All Terms Final**: All commercial terms, including pricing, scope, service levels, and special conditions, are finalized and agreed upon.
- **Financial Terms Confirmed**: Payment terms, invoicing schedule, and any special financial arrangements have been confirmed.
- **Win Reason Documented**: The reason for the win (e.g., "Customer selected for technical solution fit," "Best pricing," "Relationship strength," "Proof of concept successful") is documented in the opportunity for analysis.

**Managing Stage Gate Exceptions**:

While stage gates are strict, there may be occasional situations where a sales representative believes they should move forward despite an incomplete gate. Exceptions require documented approvals from a manager or gatekeeper. If an exception is approved, it should be recorded in the opportunity record, and any missing information should be captured as soon as possible after the stage move.

**The Value of Stage Gates**:

Stage gates ensure that:

- Deals are progressed systematically and thoughtfully, not rashly.
- Critical information is captured before moving forward.
- Approvers and leadership have confidence in forecast accuracy.
- Customers are engaged at the right time with the right information.
- Failures and delays are caught early, not late in the process.
- The entire organization has a shared understanding of what each stage means.

Respect the stage gates. They are there to protect the business and improve your chances of winning deals efficiently and accurately.
